Population aging challenge future mitigation of death attributable to PM_{2.5} pollution in China

Abstract: Estimating the health burden of air pollution in China against the background of population aging is of great significance for the well-being of elderly individuals and achieving the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Since previous studies did not fully consider the differences on drivers of deaths attributable to PM_{2.5} pollution (DAPP) (i.e., socioeconomic factors, climate factors, and disease mortalities) among provinces in China, the impact of future population aging on DAPP cannot be comprehensively and objectively estimated. In this study, we estimated and verified the spatiotemporal changes of DAPP in China from 2005 to 2017 based on a risk assessment framework. Then, using the latest climate projections, we estimated the changes and drivers in DAPP under four scenarios in China from 2018 to 2035 based on decomposition analysis. The results show that from 2017 to 2035, DAPP in China will decrease between 31.9 (26.6-45.6) thousand and 178.8 (152.5-210.5) thousand across three scenarios (sustainable development (SSP1-2.6), business as usual (SSP2-4.5), and fossil fuel development (SSP5-8.5)). We found that population aging will be the leading driver contributing to the increase in DAPP and offset the positive gains achieved by improvement in air pollution and healthcare. In the future, reducing the exposure to air pollution and improving healthcare of the elderly is necessary to mitigate the global health burden of air pollution and this requires mutual cooperation and long-term efforts among countries worldwide.

Key words: PM_{2.5} pollution, population aging, CMIP6, shared socioeconomic pathways; representative concentration pathways; public health

1. Introduction

Recently, air pollution caused by particulate matter with a diameter of 2.5 \( \mu \)m or less (i.e., PM_{2.5}) has increased dramatically^{1}. Exposure to PM_{2.5} pollution has adverse effects on human health, such as premature death and pulmonary and cardiovascular disease^{2,3}. Consequently, the number of deaths attributable to PM_{2.5} pollution (DAPP) globally has also increased rapidly, from 1.14 million to nearly 3 million between 2005 and 2017^{4}, becoming the fifth-leading mortality risk factor^{5}. In parallel, the world’s population is aging which is further exacerbating the negative health effects of air pollution^{6-8}. From 2005 to 2017, the proportion of elderly population (i.e., those aged 65 and older) rose from 7.3% to 8.6% globally, making it the fastest growing age group^{9}. It is estimated that in 2016, 69% of global air pollution-related deaths were among the elderly^{10}. In the future, the proportion of elderly population is expected to rise to 19% by 2050^{11}.

China is experiencing particularly acute population aging, with the proportion of elderly growing from 7.0% to 12.6% from 2000-2019. It is expected that by 2030 and 2050, this proportion will increase to 17.1% and 26.3%, respectively^{11}, peaking at 437 million people by 2051^{12}. The United Nations Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3 states that “people of all ages need to live
healthy lives and that a substantial reduction in air pollution-related deaths needs to be achieved by 2030\(^{13}\). Understanding the health effects of air pollution in the context of population aging in China is of great significance for promoting the well-being of elderly people and the insights gained can help other countries deal with the health burden of air pollution in the context of population aging, promoting global progress towards the SDGs.

Currently, some studies have quantified DAPP in China and focused on the impact of population aging on DAPP\(^{7,14}\). In addition to the age structure of the population, DAPP is also affected by other socioeconomic development factors (e.g., medical conditions, and air pollution emissions) and climate change (e.g., transformation and diffusion of air pollution)\(^{15,16}\). Many studies have estimated the changes of DAPP in the future considering the change of socioeconomic development or climate change\(^{17,18}\). However, we still have a limited understanding of the future dynamics of DAPP for at least two reasons. First, from the perspective of scenario setting, most studies mainly focus on the changes in DAPP under the influence of a single factor, which does not consider the driving effect of socioeconomic development and climate change on DAPP simultaneously. For example, Maji et al\(^{19}\) only considered the changes in pollutant concentration in the context of the air pollution prevention and control policy and ignored the changes in socioeconomic factors such as population and medical conditions. Shen et al\(^{20}\) studied the impact of population migration on DAPP in China and did not consider the impact of climatic factors on DAPP. Although Rafaj et al\(^{21}\) considered the impact of the age structure on DAPP, their scenario setting was mainly based on the paths of climate change, ignoring changes in other socioeconomic factors such as medical conditions. Secondly, from the perspective of the data, most studies in China did not fully account for the differences in climatic factors and socioeconomic factors among provinces, which could affect the reliability of the estimations. For example, Yue et al\(^{22}\) predicted China's DAPP in 2030 in different air pollution prevention and control policy scenarios. This scenario setting does not take into account the differences in PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration changes in different provinces in the future. In addition, the health data used by Wu et al\(^{23}\) were national-scale data obtained from the Global Burden of Disease\(^{24}\) and did not include the differences in health levels across provinces. The population data used by Liu et al\(^{17}\) are also national-scale data, ignoring regional differences in population aging in China. In summary, we still do not have a reliable and effective estimate of DAPP that accounts for the driving factors of socioeconomic pathways and changes in climate across different provinces in China.

The sixth Coupled Model Intercomparison Project (CMIP6) considers socioeconomic development as well as climate change factors and can reflect the interaction between them, providing the possibility to effectively predict DAPP in the future. CMIP6 combines representative concentration pathways (RCPs) with shared socioeconomic pathways (SSPs). Specifically, RCPs are multi-model global scenarios of greenhouse gases that use the radiative forcing per unit area at the end of the century to represent the climate impact of future greenhouse gas concentrations, covering a variety of climate mitigation levels and providing the basis for the simulation of PM\(_{2.5}\) in different climate change scenarios. SSPs are socioeconomic development scenarios related to greenhouse gas emissions that take socioeconomic aspects such as economy, population, technology, lifestyle, policies, and institutions into account, making it possible to study the socioeconomic factors affecting DAPP. In addition, Yin et al\(^{10}\) estimated disease mortality associated with air
pollution across provinces in China from 1990 to 2017, providing an important database for estimating differences in DAPP at the provincial scale. Decomposition analysis\(^{22}\) can transform the nonlinear relationship between DAPP and its various drivers into a simple linear relationship, which takes into account the interaction between the various factors. Therefore, based on the CMIP6 dataset, provincial-level disease mortality data and decomposition analysis, this paper can study the driving effects of socioeconomic changes (especially population aging) and climate change on DAPP and their differences at the provincial scale in China.

The purpose of this study is to estimate the temporal and spatial patterns of DAPP and the impact of population aging in China under alternative future scenarios considering both socioeconomic development and climate change. We first estimated and verified the spatiotemporal pattern of DAPP in China from 2005 to 2017 based on the comparable risk assessment framework. Then, we estimated the changes in DAPP in China in different scenarios from 2018 to 2035 by combining the RCPs and SSPs. Based on the results, the influence of population aging as well as other driving factors (e.g., population growth, change in PM\(_{2.5}\) concentrations and disease mortalities) were evaluated via decomposition analysis. The differences across regions are discussed and policy recommendations are made for improving public health.

2. Results

2.1 Trends in driving factors

From 2000 to 2017, China has experienced a steady increase in population by 9.8%, yet the projected population (2017-2035) varies under different scenarios (Figure 1). In the middle of the road scenario (SSP2-4.5) and the regional rivalry scenario (SSP3-7.0), the population grew from 2017, increasing by 3.0% and 5.9% in 2035, respectively. In the sustainability (SSP1-2.6) and fossil fuelled development (SSP5-8.5) scenarios, the population remained steady. The percentage of elderly people (65 years or older) continued to rise in all four scenarios, especially in SSP5-8.5 and SSP2-4.5, where it was projected to increase by 190% and 120% from 2017-2035, respectively. In SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0, the percentage of elderly people increased 110% and 90%, respectively. The population-weighted PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration in China increased by 46.6% from 2000 to 2017, while 2010 was a turning point; we only observed an increase of 0.42 ug/m\(^3\) between 2010 and 2017 (Figure 1). The estimated trends of the population-weighted PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration revealed difference between the four scenarios (Figure 1). Specifically, only SSP3-7.0 saw an increase, with the population-weighted PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration in China reaching 43.33 ug/m\(^3\) by 2035. In the other three scenarios, the population-weighted PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration was projected to decrease (2017-2035), with SSP1-2.6 experiencing the greatest decrease of 50.0%, followed by SSP2-4.5 (39.1%) and SSP5-8.5 (30.7%).

Most age-standardized death rates of diseases in different scenarios demonstrated a downward trend (Figure 1). With the joint effects of socioeconomic development and improvement in health care, most death rates of diseases saw a distinct decrease, especially in the sustainability scenario
(SSP1-2.6) and the fossil fuel development scenario (SSP5-8.5). For instance, in the SSP1-2.6 scenario, the age-standardized death rates of COPD, LRIs and stroke (2017-2035) decreased by 42.7%, 17.6% and 53.8%, respectively.

2.2 DAPP trends in China

From 2017 to 2035, DAPP in China exhibited two contrasting trends for two stages in the four scenarios (Figure 2a). From 2017-2025, annual DAPP was projected to decline, with decreases of 132 (123-146, 95% CI) thousand (SSP1-2.6), 71 (64-81, 95% CI) thousand (SSP2-4.5), 26 (19-35, 95% CI) thousand (SSP3-7.0) and 118 (103-136, 95% CI) thousand (SSP5-8.5). Conversely, from 2025-2035, under SSP2-4.5, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5, DAPP in China was projected to grow by 37 (29-44 95% CI) thousand, 72 (62-81, 95% CI) thousand and 24 (19-33, 95% CI) thousand, respectively. DAPP in the SSP1-2.6 scenario will decline with a minor decrease of 44 (37-53, 95%
CI) thousand.

Between 2017 and 2035, significant regional variation in DAPP occurred (Figure 2b). Taking the sustainability scenario (SSP1-2.6) as an example, the only increase (0.1%, 0%-0.13%) in DAPP was projected to occur in Northeast China, while in the six other regions DAPP declined. Central China showed the largest decrease (31%, 22.3% - 38.6%), while the eastern regions showed the smallest (8.8%, 3.2% - 10.7%, 95% CI). In addition, it is worth noting that between 2020 and 2035, except for the central and eastern regions where DAPP was projected to be higher than the national average value by 76.7% (64.2%-83.8%) and 29.1% (19.2%-37.8%).

Considering the changes in DAPP per capita in 2017-2035 (Figure 2c, 2d), Northeast China was again the only region expected to experience an increase (12.0%, 9.2-14.8%). In addition, DAPP per capita (2017-2035) in Northeast, Central and Northwest China was consistently above the national average value. By 2035, the DAPP per capita in Northeast China, Central China and Northwest China will reach 855, 679, and 634 people (per 10,000 people), respectively.
Figure 2 Varying trends in DAPP from 2000 to 2035.
a.) Changes in the total amount of DAPP in China; b.) Changes in the total amount of DAPP among regions; c.) Changes in DAPP per capita in China; d.) Changes in DAPP per capita among regions. The value for each region in (b) and (d) is the average value of DAPP or DAPP per capita for the provinces within that region. National average represents the average of all provinces in the country.

There will be substantial spatial differences in the projected trends in DAPP in China (Figure 2). To illustrate, we describe the two most extreme scenarios—the sustainability scenario (SSP1-2.6) with the greatest decline in DAPP and the regional rivalry scenario (SSP3-7.0) with the greatest growth (See the full results in Table S1 and Figure S1). Across regions, Henan and Hebei were projected to have the largest decrease in DAPP. In the sustainability scenario (SSP1-2.6), the decrease in DAPP in each province the DAPP reduction in the two provinces is more than 4 times to the national average level in China, and under the regional rivalry scenario (SSP3-7.0) scenario is more than 14 times to the national average.

2.3 Driving factors of DAPP
Overall, population aging is the most significant factor affecting the increase in DAPP in 2017-2035 (Figure 3). Population aging is the only factor contributing to the growth of DAPP in the sustainable development (SSP1-2.6) scenario and fossil fuel development (SSP5-8.5) scenario, resulting in increases of 674 thousand and 703 thousand DAPP per annum, respectively. In the business as usual (SSP2-4.5) and regional competition (SSP3-7.0) scenarios, although population growth will also bring a small amount of DAPP growth, more than 98% of it is due to population aging. Specifically, in these two scenarios, population aging brings increases of 730 thousand (718-746, 95% CI) and 634 (608-656, 95% CI) thousand DAPP, respectively. Meanwhile, disease mortality is an important factor alleviating the growth in DAPP, and more than 70% of the reduction in DAPP is due to the reduction in disease mortality which decreased DAPP by 615 (602-636, 95% CI) thousand, 658(608-656, 95%CI) thousand, 693 (678-726, 95% CI) thousand, and 636 (611-657, 95% CI) thousand from 2017-2035 in the four scenarios, respectively.

The amount of DAPP growth caused by population aging was projected to offset the DAPP decline caused by the decrease in disease mortality in the future and lead to an increase in DAPP. Specifically, in the four scenarios, DAPP shows a decreasing trend from 2017 to 2025, which is mainly due to the decrease in disease mortality. In this period, taking the regional competition (SSP3-7.0) scenario as an example, the reduction in disease mortality resulted in a reduction in DAPP of 371(354-396, 95%CI) thousand, which effectively offset the increase in DAPP of 281(268-301, 95%CI) thousand due to population aging. From 2025 to 2035, the age structure will become the most important factor driving the growth in DAPP. The increase in the aging population has led to an increase of 353(328-376, 95% CI) thousand DAPP, an increase of 72 (57-96, 95% CI) thousand from a decade ago. The impact of disease mortality on DAPP was projected to level off. From 2025 to 2035, the reduction in disease mortality will bring a reduction of 266 (238-276, 95% CI) thousand DAPP, which is 49(29-56, 95% CI) thousand less than ten years ago. Therefore, the impact of age structure on DAPP will begin to exceed the impact of disease mortality on DAPP and become the main reason for the increase in DAPP from 2025-2035.
Figure 3 Contributions of different factors to DAPP changes in China from 2017 to 2035

The contributions of different drivers to DAPP showed certain differences at the provincial scale (Figure 4). For regions with higher rates of aging, such as Heilongjiang and Jilin, age structure was the most important factor affecting the change in DAPP. In these regions, the increase in DAPP attributable to population aging exceeded 50% of the national average. Affected by the aging of the population, the total DAPP in these regions continued to rise under all four scenarios, and the total increase was 2 times the national average. Taking Heilongjiang Province with the highest level of aging population as an example, in the four scenarios, the total DAPP will increase by 10,000, 6,000, 10,000 and 3,000, respectively, which will be 1.2 times, 6.7 times, 5.8 times and 1.8 times higher than the average change in the national DAPP. In Tibet, Ningxia, and Guizhou, where the proportion of elderly population is lower, the growth in DAPP caused by population aging was projected to be less than half of the average change in the national DAPP. Although the reduction in DAPP caused by factors such as the improvement of medical conditions in these regions is also lower than the average of all provinces in the country, overall, the DAPP in these regions still shows a downward trend.

In addition, regarding types of diseases and considering all age groups affected by DAPP, the older individuals (65 years or older) are still the most vulnerable group to DAPP (Figure 5). By
2035, in any scenario, the average proportion of older people dying from six diseases (i.e., chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer, lower respiratory infection, ischemic heart disease, stroke and diabetes mellitus type 2) will account for more than 80% of the total DAPP. Specifically, the percentage of these individuals with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and lower respiratory infection will be larger than 95% and 94%, respectively. Furthermore, the percentage of DAPP in older individuals for all six diseases will rise steadily from 2017 to 2035. Among them, the percentage attributable to lung cancer will increase the most, growing from 66% in 2017 to 81% in 2035 under SSP1-2.6, or showing an average increase of 23% in the four scenarios. For ischemic heart disease and diabetes mellitus type 2, the increase in the percentage will exceed 20%.

3. Discussion

3.1 Population aging is the most important challenge in controlling DAPP

Population aging is the leading challenge China faces in dealing with the problem of DAPP in the future. Our study found that population aging will be the primary factor driving an increase in DAPP from 2017-2035 (Figure 6). First, although the proportion of the elderly population is relatively small, the elderly will account for a large fraction of total DAPP. Between 2017 and 2035, while the proportion of elderly people will range from 10% to 35% in most regions, more than 70%(e.g., 95% in Northeast China) of the growth of DAPP will be caused by population aging.
These estimates are also in line with previous studies. For example, Bu et al\(^{25}\) found that the elderly have been affected disproportionately affected by DAPP from 1990-2017 on a global scale, and Wang et al\(^{26}\) attributed DAPP growth in China by 2020 and 2030 largely to population aging. Second, population aging will be the only driver of the increase in DAPP for most scenarios, except for the regional rivalry scenario (SSP3-7.5), in which population growth and air pollution will also contribute (approximately 2% only) to the increase in DAPP. In addition, although improvements in air quality and health care can decrease DAPP, DAPP was projected to grow in Northeast China which has the highest rate of population aging (Figure 9f).

In addition, combined with the results of this study and previous studies, the DAPP-related challenges posed by population aging are mainly demonstrated in the following two aspects. First, the adverse impact of air pollution on people’s health will be enhanced with age. Since humans experience weakening physiological and metabolic processes when getting older8 and people are exposed to poisonous substances in ambient conditions that are absorbed by and then accumulate in the human body27, older people are the most vulnerable to air pollution. Our findings show that by 2035, the average proportion of elderly people dying of diabetes mellitus type 2 and ischemic heart disease will account for over 80% of the respective total deaths of all ages. In addition, among six diseases related to DAPP, the proportions of deaths of older people will consistently increase, and three types of diseases (e.g., lung cancer) will increase by over 20%. Third, with the fast-growing aging population in China, it is projected28 that by 2022, China will enter a stage where the aging population will account for more than 14% of the total population. By 2035, the proportion of those individuals who are 65 years old will account for 20% of the total population, further intensifying the situation. The aging speed in China has exceeded that in most developed countries29. As population aging continues, DAPP is likely to subsequently increase30.

In addition to directly causing the growth of DAPP, the aging of the population may also cause the increase of DAPP through the intermediating effect, by affecting the proportion of fossil energy consumption and the proportion of household medical and health expenditures. To this end, referring to existing studies, we used the KAYA model to verify the mediating effect of population aging on DAPP via energy consumptions and health expenditures (see Supplementary Texts 1 and Table S2 for details). The findings also confirm that population aging can negatively impact health by affecting fossil energy consumption and exacerbating air pollution. This is partly due to the fact that the elderly prefer indoor activities and/or heating in winter31,32, and partly the aging population may inhibit technological innovation33. In addition, the aging of the population will also increase the proportion of medical and health expenditure in the total household consumption by affecting the increase in DAPP. This means that the aging of the population will increase the pressure on household consumption, which is not conducive to the prevention and control of DAPP. This is also consistent with the results of existing studies7,34.

Hence, to alleviate the health burden caused by air pollution and decrease health risks, apart from improving air quality, China should pay special attention to older people who are directly exposed to air pollution. First, formulating plans for promoting public health awareness is suggested. This will encourage elderly individuals to conduct indoor activities during adverse weather conditions, thus reducing direct exposure to polluted air. For example, the U.S.A. and European
Union countries have implemented public health awareness plans\(^8\) to provide information on air pollution for elderly individuals, encouraging them to reduce their activities in highly contaminated conditions. Second, the government should improve health care by supporting additional investments in health care and adopting targeted measures to improve older people’s health care insurance and alleviate their economic burden. Other policies, such as providing people residing in polluted areas with medical compensation, incorporating medicines targeted at compulsory diseases into medical insurance and reducing the medical cost of treating air pollution-related diseases, are suggested as well. Studies\(^{27}\) have shown that assisting older people suffering from lung cancer and ischemic heart disease with economic support is effective in relieving the adverse health impacts of air pollution.

3.2 International insight on DAPP Prevention and Control in different regions of China

With a rapidly aging society and room left to improve air quality and health care in Central, West and Northeast China, DAPP prevention and control in these regions is facing great challenges. Specifically, in Central and West China, in addition to the continuous increase in the proportion of the elderly population will hinder the prevention and control of DAPP, inferior medical treatment and environmental quality contribute a great deal. Zhang et al discussed the weak regulations on public health and environmental quality due to poor regional economic development\(^{35}\). Therefore, compared to the eastern regions, air pollution in poorer areas produced greater adverse effects on health. In Northeast China, population aging was the main driving factor in DAPP growth. In Heilongjiang and Jilin, the amount of DAPP caused by population aging equaled nearly twice the average amount among provinces. Confronted with high rates of population aging, even the improvement of health care and environmental quality could not reverse the DAPP trend, thus causing DAPP to grow consistently\(^{10}\). Although Central and Northeast China are faced with great challenges regarding DAPP prevention and control, Eastern, Southeast and Southwestern China have had some successes in this regard. The way of air pollution prevents and control and the equipped health care system in the area have set an imitable example for other regions. Kamal et al\(^{19}\) pointed out that the Air Pollution Prevention and Control Action Plan issued by the Chinese government makes it possible to contain PM\(_{2.5}\) pollution in certain cities, such as Shanghai and Guangzhou. Through the subsequent implementation of the plan, it is projected that by 2030, 280,000 premature deaths could be prevented. In terms of other socioeconomic aspects, Jiao et al\(^{36}\) found that in cities with better education and medical resources, such as Beijing and Shanghai, residents have better health awareness and public health insurance, thus offsetting the adverse effect caused by air pollution to some degree.

The future trends in DAPP in different regions of China are informative for other countries and regions (Table 1). First, air pollution poses the biggest threat to health in less economically developed regions such as Central and West China. In the assessment of air pollution and health
from the WHO\(^{37}\) report, nearly 90% of air pollution-related deaths occurred in low-income and middle-income countries (e.g., South Asian and Western Pacific countries). Confined due to limitations in technology levels, most developing countries have substantial air pollution\(^{38}\). Therefore, these countries should draw lessons from developed countries in terms of adjusting their industrial structure and exploiting green energy to reduce air pollution, as well as improving health care levels.

Second, for countries and regions that have achieved preliminary economic development and improvement in air quality and health care (e.g., Northeast China), the threat of population aging was the main culprit for the increase in DAPP. Therefore, on the premise of establishing a complete system of health care, these countries and regions should pay special attention to providing medical help and perfecting basic pension plans for vulnerable people such as elderly individuals. For instance, Beard et al\(^{31}\) suggested that countries and regions should establish long-term nursing systems to fulfill the requirements of older people in all aspects of health care. In addition, governments should provide health services in regions adjacent to older people's residences, especially those live in polluted areas, guarantee their accessibility to vaccines and other medical products, and conduct targeted training for professional nursing personnel. Lloyd-Sherlock et al\(^{32}\) specifically focused on sub-Saharan African countries, identifying local governments that adopted measures to improve the health care of older people in the face of population aging.

Third, for regions and countries with high-level economic development (a region such as East China), due to improvements in air quality and health care facilities, the decrease in DAPP brought by lowering the death rates of diseases and improving air quality can effectively offset the increase in DAPP led by population aging. For countries and regions such as the U.S.A.\(^{39}\) and the European Union\(^{40,41}\), continuously optimizing the health care system and further improving air quality are effective for controlling DAPP. In addition, conducting regional collaboration is encouraged to jointly address air pollution, develop a green economy and reduce health risks. For example, European countries have developed emissions reduction technology and encourage green business to reduce industrial pollution, thus reducing health risks\(^{40}\). In the case of China, Wu et al\(^{42}\) also pointed out that regions such as the Yangtze River Delta (Shanghai, Jiangsu and Zhejiang) should collaborate frequently to tackle air pollution, thus maximizing the benefits. Ji et al\(^{43}\) also pointed out that creating more green space can help prolong life and cope with China's aging society.

In summary, with the aging population increasing globally, the prevention and control of DAPP has become a common challenge faced by all countries worldwide. From 2000-2016, the proportion of people 60 years or older grew by 50%\(^7\). The growing proportion of older people is likely to add additional health costs brought by air pollution, making it more difficult to control DAPP and to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) over time. Therefore, it requires mutual cooperation and long-term continuous efforts among countries around the world to take actions to improve air quality, reduce the exposure of elderly individuals to air pollution, and improve health care for older people. On the one hand, immediate action is needed to improve air quality, adhere to a sustainable development path, and avoid the patterns of regional competition and burning of fossil fuels. This requires countries around the world to make efforts to reduce pollutant emissions from the source. At the same time, considering the fluidity of air pollution, cooperation in air pollution control will help all countries around the world jointly improve environmental quality, reducing the health risks from air pollution. On the other hand, attention needs to be paid to the health of the aging population. It is necessary to increase medical and health investment for the elderly population and to take more targeted measures to prevent elderly individuals from being exposed to air pollution and to take necessary measures to adjust the population structure to manage the aging of the population.

4 Conclusions

From 2017 to 2025, the trend in DAPP in China is estimated to decline by 19 -146 thousand (95% CI) in all four scenarios. From 2025-2035, the DAPP will begin to increase by 19-81 thousand (95% CI) in the three scenarios except the sustainable development scenario (SSP1-2.6), in which
the DAPP will decrease by 44 thousand (37-53, 95% CI). The changes in DAPP among the provinces of China will exhibit substantial differences. Taking the sustainable development (SSP1-2.6) scenario as an example, from 2017 to 2035, East China will be the region with the least reduction in DAPP, while Central China will be the region with the largest reduction in DAPP, 231(211-253, 95% CI) thousand, nearly 7 times more than East China.

Overall, from 2017 to 2035, population aging will be the most important driver of DAPP growth. In the sustainable development (SSP1-2.6) and fossil fuel development (SSP5-8.5) scenarios, population aging is the only factor leading to DAPP growth in 2017-2035, resulting in increases of 718-746 (95% CI) thousand and 608-656 (95% CI) thousand DAPP, respectively. In the business as usual (SSP2-4.5) and regional competition (SSP3-7.0) scenarios, more than 98% (95% CI) of DAPP growth is due to population aging.

In the future, with the fast growth of global aging, the prevention and control of DAPP will require more effort from all countries to achieve the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals. On the one hand, it is necessary to pay attention to the health problems caused by population aging, increase medical and health investment for the elderly population, separate heavy industry from where people (especially the elderly) live and take more targeted measures to avoid the exposure of the elderly population to air pollution. In addition, immediate action is needed to improve air quality, adhere to a sustainable development path, and avoid scenarios involving regional competition and the burning of fossil fuels. All these require mutual cooperation and the long-term efforts of all countries around the world.
Materials and methods

Study area

This paper focuses on the change in and driving factors of DAPP in China at the provincial scale. The GBD study showed that PM_{2.5} pollution caused 4.2 million deaths worldwide in 2015, of which China was the country with the largest number of deaths, reaching 1.11 million (IHME and HEI, 2017). In addition, China is the country with the largest elderly population in the world (United Nations, 2017). By the end of 2017, the population aged over 60 years in China was 240 million, accounting for 25% of the world's elderly population.

This study assessed 31 provinces, autonomous regions, and municipalities, with Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan excluded due to data limitations. The 31 provinces were aggregated into North, Northeast, East, Central, South, Southwest, and Northwest regions (Appendix Figure S2). From 2000 to 2017, the population-weighted PM_{2.5} concentration and population aging in China showed an upward trend. The average population-weighted atmospheric PM_{2.5} concentration in China increased from 28.1 ug/cm^3 to 41.1 ug/cm^3, an increase of 46.3% (Global PM_{2.5} Assessment Dataset).

Data

The data used in this study includes pollutant concentration data, disease mortality data, historical socioeconomic data, air pollutant composition data, future socioeconomic data, and other geographic data (Table 2).

Future PM_{2.5} composition data came from the CMIP6 dataset. Specifically, the data are based on the emissions, climate and meteorological specification under each scenario in the ScenarioMIP framework and are logically consistent with socioeconomic factors in the SSPs^{46}. We used the average of the simulation results of multiple models for each scenario^{49}.

Future socioeconomic data include data on China’s population age structure and population education level at the provincial scale^{48} and data on the total population at 0.5° grid cell resolution^{47}. Based on the latest provincial and prefecture-level city statistical yearbook data and population data in the RCP pathways, the dataset from Chen et al^{29} constructs the future population of China's provinces in five shared socioeconomic pathways from 2010 to 2100 for the first time and depicts the different developments of future economic and social systems. The data reflects the internally consistent relationships and logic of socio-economic development and climate change at the provincial scale. With 2010 as the base year, Jiang et al^{47} accumulated data on key elements such as fertility, mortality, and migration rates and estimated the annual population out to 2100 under each SSP.

*Estimate and validate the spatiotemporal pattern of DAPP in China from 2005 to 2017*

First, we used the comparable risk assessment framework^{24} to calculate DAPP^{50} as a function of the population attributable fraction (PAF), population, disease mortality and age structure^{37,51}:

\[
DAPP = \sum_{a,d}(PAF_{a,d} \times POP \times Rate_{a,d} \times AgePa)
\] (1)

where PAF_{a,d} refers to the attributable fraction of disease d in the population of age a, POP refers to the population, Rate_{a,d} refers to the mortality rate of disease d in the people of age a, and AgePa refers to the proportion of the population of age a in the total population. Among them, age a includes 15 stages: 25-30, 30-35, ..., 90-95, and >95 years old. Disease d includes 6 categories: chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, infectious lower respiratory tract infection, lung cancer, ischemic heart disease, stroke and type 2 diabetes^{37}. In addition, PAF_{a,d} is determined by the relative risk \( RR_{a,d} \) of death due to disease d in age group a^{37}. Specifically, it can be expressed as^{5},

\[
PAF_{a,d} = \frac{RR_{a,d}-1}{RR_{a,d}}
\] (2)

when the PM_{2.5} exposure level is higher, the \( RR_{a,d} \) will be higher^{24,52}. The quantitative relationship between them can be obtained from the exposure-response function. The exposure-response function used in this study comes from the MR-BRT model recently announced in GBD2019^{24}, and the DAPP estimated based on the median value of the MR-BRT model as the primary result^{24}.

We used Pearson's correlation to verify the reliability of the results (Appendix Figure S3). Specifically, we compared the results obtained in Equation (1) with the estimates calculated by Yue et al^{22} based on the Integrated Exposure Response (IER) model, the estimates calculated by Geng et al^{14} based on the Global Exposure Mortality Model (GEMM), and the estimates calculated Yin et
Estimate DAPP in China from 2017 to 2035

We calculated future the PM_{2.5} concentration in China based on empirical formulas and future pollutant concentration data. We used the GBD model to estimate future disease mortality in China and quantified DAPP based on a comparable risk assessment framework.

Specifically, referring to the study of Chowdhury et al^{53} and Van Donkelaar et al^{54}, we used the empirical formula to estimate PM_{2.5} concentration:

\[
\text{PM}_{2.5} = \text{BC} + \text{OA} + SO_4 + NH_4 + 0.25 \times SS + 0.1 \times dust
\] (3)

\[
NH_4 = \frac{(36 \times SO_4)}{96}
\] (4)

where BC represents black carbon, OA represents organic aerosol, and SS represents sea salt. Since the CMIP6 does not report the concentration of surface NH_4, we assumed that NH_4 exists only in the form of ammonium sulfate, and the concentration of NH_4 was estimated from the concentration of SO_4.

Since the PM_{2.5} concentration obtained from the simulation is lower than the actual monitoring results, we calibrated the PM_{2.5} concentration calculated by the model and the observation results of the baseline period (2012-2017) as follows:

\[
PM_{2.5,pentad=x}^{mod} = PM_{2.5,sat,pentad=bl} + PM_{2.5,sat,pentad=bl} \times \frac{PM_{2.5,model,pentad=x} - PM_{2.5,model,pentad=bl}}{PM_{2.5,model,pentad=bl}}
\] (5)

where \( PM_{2.5,sat,pentad=bl} \) represents the PM_{2.5} concentration during the baseline period (2012-2017), \( PM_{2.5,model,pentad=x} \) represents the PM_{2.5} concentration obtained according to equation 5, and \( PM_{2.5,model,pentad=bl} \) represents the PM_{2.5} concentration during the baseline period obtained from equation 5,.

In addition, for future disease mortality, we used the GBD development prediction model to estimate it according to the study by Foreman et al^{55}. According to the model, the following relationship is satisfied between disease mortality and its drivers:

\[
ln(m) \sim N(\hat{y} + \hat{\epsilon}, \sigma)
\] (6)

where m represents the mortality rate of a specific disease, whose value is mainly determined by \( \hat{y} \) (representing variables such as long-term trends, socioeconomic development, and risk factors) and \( \hat{\epsilon} \) (representing residuals that cannot be explained by the above factors).

\[
\hat{y} = \beta_1 SDI_{<0.8} + \beta_2 SDI_{\geq0.8} + \theta_a t + \alpha_{la}
\] (7)

\[
\hat{\epsilon} = ARIMA\left( \epsilon_{history} \right)
\] (8)

where \( \beta 0 \) and \( \beta 1 \) represent the global coefficients when the sociodemographic index (SDI) is less
than 0.8 and greater than 0.8, respectively. \( \theta_a \) represents the long-term trend for a specific age group, and t represents time. \( \alpha_{1a} \) refers to the intercept for a specific age in a specific region. \( \epsilon^* \) can be calculated by the autoregressive integrated moving average function.

Finally, based on the estimated results of PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration and disease mortality as well as the population and age structure data, we quantified the DAPP from 2018 to 2035 with the comparable risk assessment framework model of Formula (1). In addition, the upper and lower bounds of the confidence interval for DAPP in this study were determined by the upper and lower bounds of the exposure response function and the upper and lower bounds of confidence intervals for PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration and disease mortality. Among them, the upper and lower limits of the 95% confidence interval range of the exposure-response function are the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles of each result in the 1000th curve fit of the meta-regression—the Bayesian, regularized, trimmed (MR-BRT) model\(^{24}\). The confidence interval of the PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration was determined by the difference of 11 models in the CMIP6 dataset\(^{53}\). This study assumed that the simulation results of each model conformed to the t-distribution\(^{46}\), and the 95% confidence interval of the PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration can be expressed as:

\[
C_{CI} = Con_{mean} \pm \sqrt{Var(C)} * T_{95\%}(n-1)
\]

where \( C_{CI} \) represents the uncertainty interval of the PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration, \( Con_{mean} \) refers to the mean value of the PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration obtained by multiple models, and \( Var(C) \) refers to the variance in the PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration obtained from multiple models. \( T_{95\%(n-1)} \) represents the t-statistic with n-1 degrees of freedom under the 95% confidence interval, where n is the number of correlated modes.

Disease mortality is determined by \( \epsilon \) in the linear model Formula (8)\(^{55}\), \( \epsilon \) represents the residual between the estimated value and the actual value, obeying the mean of 0, and the variance of \( Var(e_0) \) is normally distributed. Therefore, the 95% confidence interval for ln(m) can be expressed as:

\[
\ln(m)_{CI} = \hat{y} + \hat{\epsilon} \pm \sqrt{Var(\epsilon - \hat{\epsilon})} * T_{95\%}(n-2)
\]

where \( \ln(m)_{CI} \) represents the uncertainty interval for the natural logarithm of mortality from a specific type and age group of diseases, the sum of y* and \( \epsilon^* \) represents the predicted value of \( \ln(m) \), and \( Var(\epsilon - \hat{\epsilon}) \) refers to the variance of the residual term in the regression model (Equation 3.10). \( T_{95\%(n-2)} \) represents the t-statistic with n-2 degrees of freedom under the 95% confidence interval, where n is the sample size for constructing the regression model. \( Var(\epsilon - \hat{\epsilon}) \) can be further obtained by the following formula.

\[
Var(\epsilon - \hat{\epsilon}) = \frac{\sum e_i^2}{n-2} \times \left[ \frac{1}{n} \times \frac{(x_f-\bar{x})^2}{(x_i-\bar{x})^2} \right]
\]

where \( \sum e_i^2 \) is the residual sum of squares of the regression model, \( x_i \) and \( \bar{x} \) represent the mean of each independent variable and independent variable in the regression model, respectively, and \( x_f \) represents the input value of the independent variable when predicting the future based on the regression model.

We further compared the DAPP calculated in this paper of historical periods with previous
studies to verify the rationality of the quantitative method. We found that previous studies\(^{10,14,22}\) showed similar results to ours. Although some estimates based on the GEMM model were higher than our estimates\(^{14}\), the results showed a similar trend over time. This is because the RR estimated by GEMM model are higher compared to the RR values obtained by the IER function, especially in areas with high PM\(_{2.5}\) concentrations\(^{7}\). Also, we compared the results estimated under different future scenarios with the results estimated by GBDMAPS (Table S3). Different from the four scenarios in this study, the results in GBDMAPS was mainly based on different emission pathways under different energy utilization and air pollution control policies. Consistent with our findings, DAPP were lower under the sustainable development paths with policy interventions (such as the BAU2 and PC2 scenarios in GBDMAPS China and the SSP1-2.6 scenarios in this study). However, since we only considered outdoor air pollution when quantifying DAPP, ignoring indoor pollution and its interaction with outdoor air pollution, the amount of DAPP in this study is lower than the results of GBDMAPS China. In addition, the disease mortality data used in GBDMAPS was simulated based on the estimated value of GBD2013. It was assumed that the change trend of disease mortality is consistent with the change trend from 1990 to 2013, and the changes in socioeconomic levels such as medical conditions across different scenarios were not taken into account. Therefore, the disease mortality in GBDMAPS China were higher than the ones in our study. This is another reason for the higher estimated DAPP estimated by GBDMAPS than our estimates.

*Analyze the drivers of DAPP by the decomposition method*

In addition to being affected by the age structure of the population, DAPP is also affected by the effects of population size, PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration and related disease mortality. Specifically, the total population and proportion of aged population, PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration, and disease mortality are all positively correlated with DAPP. Based on Cohen et al\(^{5}\) and Yue et al\(^{22}\), we quantified the influence of age structure and population size, PM\(_{2.5}\) concentration and related disease mortality on DAPP using the decomposition method. The advantage of decomposition method is to transform the nonlinear relationship between individual drivers and DAPP changes into a simple linear relationship\(^{5}\). Compared with simple sensitivity analysis, decomposition method takes into account the interaction of various factors, which makes the number of DAPP correspond to the contribution of driving factors. The net change of DAPP in a certain period of time can be expressed as the sum of the contributions of various driving factors to the change of DAPP, which is helpful for the quantitative analysis of the driving mechanism of DAPP. At the same time, the decomposition method converts the changes of different driving factors into contributions to the changes of DAPP, which supports the judgment of the relative importance of each factor.

In the decomposition analysis (Appendix Figure S4), we gradually introduced the
changes in age structure, population, PM_{2.5} concentration and disease mortality separately to calculate DAPP. The difference between the pre-introduction and post-introduction results is the contribution of age structure. In addition, considering that the order of introduction can affect the calculation results, this study calculated all 24 possible combinations of the order of the introduction and used the average of the contributions of the four drivers in each case as the final result. Based on the decomposition analysis, we calculated the driving factors of DAPP in China in 2017-2025, 2025-2030, and 2030-2035 and then analyzed the synergistic driving effect of age structure and other factors on DAPP.

[ANSWER]: This paper’s methodology for projecting disease mortality and applying the GBD exposure–response function lacks the necessary operational details (drivers, coefficients, and risk-curve specifications), preventing independent reproduction of DAPP results and weakening confidence in the robustness of the projected scenario differences.

"In addition, for future disease mortality, we used the GBD development prediction model to estimate it according to the study by Foreman et al\$^{55}$."
This sentence claims reliance on a specific, complex forecasting framework, but the manuscript does not provide enough implementation detail to allow replication at the provincial level. In practice, applying the Foreman et al. framework requires specifying which covariates/drivers are used, how they are measured for each province, and how coefficients are obtained or transferred. The paper presents generic equations but does not provide parameter values (\(\beta\), \(\theta_a\), \(\alpha\)) or the underlying time series used to fit the models. Without these, other researchers cannot reproduce the mortality trajectories that materially influence DAPP projections and decomposition results. This is not a minor documentation issue: mortality projections are one of the four principal drivers the paper decomposes, so missing details directly obstruct verification of the paper’s central claim that aging offsets air-quality and health-care improvements. Transparent reporting is standard in forecasting studies precisely because different modelling choices can yield materially different mortality trends.
"According to the model, the following relationship is satisfied between disease mortality and its drivers:\n\n\[\nln(m) \sim N(\hat{y} + \hat{\epsilon}, \sigma)\n\] (6)\n\n\[\n\hat{y} = \beta_1 SDI_{<0.8} + \beta_2 SDI_{\geq0.8} + \theta_a t + \alpha_{la}\n\] (7)\n\n\[\n\hat{\epsilon} = ARIMA\left( \epsilon_{history} \right)\n\] (8)"
These equations are too high-level to be reproducible without specifying the exact definition of SDI in the study (including its components and provincial time series), the thresholding rule application, the estimation procedure for coefficients, and the ARIMA model orders/selection. The paper also does not clarify whether these equations are fitted separately for each cause-age-province combination or pooled, which determines forecast behavior and uncertainty. Moreover, the manuscript does not indicate how risk factors (beyond SDI) are incorporated, even though the cited forecasting framework is typically risk-factor driven and scenario-based. Because DAPP is computed as a product involving cause- and age-specific mortality rates, small methodological differences in mortality projection can lead to large differences in attributable deaths over time. This lack of specificity materially affects both reproduccing numerical results and assessing robustness of scenario comparisons. As a result, the mortality component of the decomposition analysis cannot be independently verified.
"Our modelling framework used data from the GBD 2016 study to systematically account for the relationships between risk factors and health outcomes for 79 independent drivers of health." [[4]](#ref4)
This sentence from Foreman et al. clarifies that the referenced approach is not just a simple SDI-plus-trend model; it explicitly incorporates a large set of drivers linking risk factors to outcomes. The manuscript under review does not describe which of these drivers are used (if any), how they are mapped to provincial data, or how alternative SSP scenarios influence them, creating a mismatch between the cited method’s requirements and the information provided. If the paper is using a materially simplified variant, it should clearly specify the simplifications and justify them, because simplification can change projected mortality trends and uncertainties. This matters for the paper’s conclusions because it attributes a large share of DAPP reductions to declining disease mortality and then argues that aging offsets these gains. Without a transparent, reproducible mortality projection consistent with the cited platform, the driver decomposition is difficult to trust quantitatively. The quote thus supports that essential methodological details are missing relative to common practice in the referenced forecasting literature.
"Theoretical Minimum Risk Exposure Level (TMREL) Draws [CSV]" [[5]](#ref5)
This excerpt from the official GBD 2019 particulate matter risk-curve record indicates that applying GBD-style PM\$_{2.5}$\ risk functions requires specifying a theoretical minimum risk exposure level (TMREL), not only an exposure–response curve shape. In the manuscript, RR is referenced via MR-BRT and used to form PAF, but the operational details of how exposure is translated into RR (including any TMREL choice and curve parameters) are not spelled out. Without specifying these inputs, two implementations can yield different attributable fractions even when using the same exposure data. This creates an avoidable reproducibility gap for a core part of the risk assessment pipeline. The issue is particularly important in future scenarios because changes in exposure distributions near TMREL can strongly affect incremental attributable risk. The evidence supports that key, implementation-critical specifications expected in GBD-style analyses are absent.

References:
[4] <a id="ref4"></a>[Foreman, K. J. et al. Forecasting life expectancy, years of life lost, and all-cause and cause-specific mortality for 250 causes of death… *The Lancet* (2018). (PubMed: 30340847)](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30340847/)
[5] <a id="ref5"></a>[IHME GHDx: Global Burden of Disease Study 2019 (GBD 2019) Particulate Matter Risk Curves (2021)](https://ghdx.healthdata.org/record/ihme-data/global-burden-disease-study-2019-gbd-2019-particulate-matter-risk-curves)

prompt.content: You are an evaluator of expert-domain scientific writing. You will get a query-answer pair along with criteria explaining the specific evaluation aspect and the scoring rubric. You should evaluate whether the answer satisfy the query based on the given criteria. First output your reasoning enclosed between <reasoning> and </reasoning>. Then output the three axis labels using the tags <correctness>, <significance>, and <evidence>. Use exact label strings from the criteria. For cascade-skipped axes, write null inside the tag. Finally, output your 10-class prediction enclosed between <score> and </score>. Inside <score> provide only the numeric class id (1-10) and nothing else.

[QUERY]: You are a meta-reviewer for scientific papers. You will be given a paper's content AND one reconstructed review item written by a reviewer (human or AI). For this review item, you must: (1) Judge the item along three axes: correctness, significance, and evidence sufficiency (your own meta-review judgment). (2) Predict how two independent expert meta-reviewers would jointly judge the item, expressed as one of 10 collapsed class labels that encode both the cascade outcome and inter-expert agreement. You are NOT writing a new review. You are judging an existing review item by verifying its claims against the paper. [CRITERIA]: ### Principles (ordered by importance) 1. Correctness -- judge the main point, not peripheral details. If the reviewer's core concern is valid even though one specific supporting claim is inaccurate, the item is still Correct. Only mark Not Correct when the main point itself is wrong. 2. Significance -- the bar is "would this improve the paper?" Any criticism that would genuinely help the paper if addressed is Significant -- it does NOT need to threaten the paper's validity. Missing statistics, undefined figure annotations, unreported methodological details, internal inconsistencies between text and figures, and missing ablations are typically Significant. Only pure stylistic preferences and trivial typos are Marginally Significant. 3. Evidence -- verifiability, not exhaustiveness. If a meta-reviewer can verify the reviewer's claim from what the reviewer wrote plus the paper, the evidence is Sufficient. When the reviewer's criticism IS that something is absent, identifying the specific absence IS the evidence. Reserve Requires More for cases where the meta-reviewer cannot even locate what part of the paper the reviewer is talking about. ### Decision procedure (follow for EACH item) Part A -- Your initial judgment (axis labels) Step 1 (Understand). Read the item. What is the main point? Step 2 (Correctness). Verify the MAIN POINT against the paper. - Core concern valid even if a peripheral detail is off? -> "Correct". Continue to Step 3. - Main point itself factually wrong? -> "Not Correct". Set significance and evidence to null. Step 3 (Significance). Would addressing this criticism genuinely improve the paper? - Would fixing it make the paper substantively better? -> "Significant" - Minor presentation or stylistic issue that does not affect substance? -> "Marginally Significant" - Trivial enough that the review would be better without it? -> "Not Significant". Set evidence to null. Step 4 (Evidence). Can a meta-reviewer verify the claim from the review item + the paper without substantial extra work? - Yes -> "Sufficient" - No -> "Requires More" Part B -- Devil's advocate + expert disagreement prediction Expert disagreement is common in meta-review -- it is not a rare edge case. For each axis, you must argue the other side before deciding whether experts would agree. Step 5 (Correctness -- devil's advocate). - If "Not Correct" in Step 2: argue the reviewer's defense. Could a charitable expert still find the core concern valid? If plausible -> predict "disagree_on_correctness" (and you may revise Step 2 to "Correct" if the defense convinces you). - If "Correct" in Step 2: argue the prosecution. Could a skeptical expert find the claim factually wrong? If plausible -> predict "disagree_on_correctness". - If neither side is plausible -> experts agree on correctness. Step 6 (Significance -- devil's advocate). Only if Step 5 = agree on Correct. - If "Significant": argue for downgrading. Could an expert see this as merely Marginally Significant? - If "Marginally Significant": argue for upgrading. Could an expert see this as genuinely improving the paper? - If counter-argument plausible -> predict "correct_disagree_on_significance". IMPORTANT: Correctness disagreement means the factual truth of the claim is ambiguous. Significance disagreement means the fact is clear but its importance is debatable. Do not mislabel correctness uncertainty as significance uncertainty. Step 7 (Evidence boundary). Only if Step 6 = agree on >= Marginal. Is evidence clearly sufficient or clearly lacking, or borderline? - Firm -> experts agree. Pick the matching agreement class. - Borderline -> "correct_significant_disagree_on_evidence" or "correct_marginal_disagree_on_evidence". ### Consistency constraint (critical) Your prediction_of_expert_judgments MUST be consistent with your FINAL axis labels (after any Part B revisions): - If final correctness = "Correct", the prediction must be a "correct_*" class OR "disagree_on_correctness". Never "incorrect". - If final correctness = "Not Correct", the prediction must be "incorrect" OR "disagree_on_correctness". Never a "correct_*" class. - Similar logic applies downstream: significance and evidence labels constrain which agreement/disagreement classes are valid. ### The 10 valid prediction_of_expert_judgments labels (map to numeric score) 1: correct_significant_sufficient 2: correct_significant_insufficient 3: correct_significant_disagree_on_evidence 4: correct_marginal_sufficient 5: correct_marginal_insufficient 6: correct_marginal_disagree_on_evidence 7: correct_not_significant 8: correct_disagree_on_significance 9: incorrect 10: disagree_on_correctness Use exact axis label strings in your reasoning and tags: "Correct" / "Not Correct"; "Significant" / "Marginally Significant" / "Not Significant"; "Sufficient" / "Requires More". Use null for cascade-skipped fields. Evidence axis "Requires More" maps to the "*_insufficient" class names above. ### Tips 1. The paper text may contain OCR errors. Do not penalize reviewers for pointing out things that are actually OCR artifacts; infer content from context. 2. Do not try to read or invent every supplementary/code file -- focus on what is provided in [PAPER] and what the review item explicitly references. 3. Apply the same significance bar consistently. Do not be lenient on one item style and strict on another. 4. Your judgment must be independent of who wrote the review. Do not infer reviewer identity (human/AI) from writing style. 5. Judge the item solely from the paper content provided below. Do not rely on external published versions, referee reports, or outside webpages about this paper. ### Output requirement for this evaluation format In , include: (1) axis judgment + why, (2) devil's advocate argument for correctness (and significance if applicable) and whether plausible, (3) the resulting expert-agreement prediction and chosen class. Aim for 4-6 sentences. Then output exactly these tags: ... ... ... Then output: ... Inside provide only the numeric class id (1-10) and nothing else. Scoring rubric is as follows. 1: correct_significant_sufficient (class 1) 2: correct_significant_insufficient (class 2) 3: correct_significant_disagree_on_evidence (class 3) 4: correct_marginal_sufficient (class 4) 5: correct_marginal_insufficient (class 5) 6: correct_marginal_disagree_on_evidence (class 6) 7: correct_not_significant (class 7) 8: correct_disagree_on_significance (class 8) 9: incorrect (class 9) 10: disagree_on_correctness (class 10) [PAPER TITLE]: First in vivo images of the human brain revealed with the Iseult 11.7T MRI scanner [PAPER]: # Abstract The understanding of the human brain is one of the main scientific challenges of the 21st century. In this context, in the early 2000s the French Atomic Energy Commission (CEA) launched a program to conceive and build the first human MRI scanner operating at 11.7T. More than a decade of developments followed to deliver the magnet while six more years were necessary to complete the commissioning and finally obtain approval from the regulatory agencies to acquire the first ever human brain images in vivo at such magnetic field. We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate. To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects. The unprecedented field strength combined with the acquisition techniques deployed yield T2 and T2*-weighted images reaching mesoscale resolutions within short acquisition times and with a high signal and contrast to noise ratio. - [Biological sciences/Biological techniques/Imaging/Magnetic resonance imaging](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FBiological%20techniques%2FImaging%2FMagnetic%20resonance%20imaging) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Diseases of the nervous system](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FDiseases%20of%20the%20nervous%20system) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Cognitive neuroscience](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FCognitive%20neuroscience) - [Biological sciences/Neuroscience/Computational neuroscience](/browse?subjectArea=Biological%20sciences%2FNeuroscience%2FComputational%20neuroscience) - [Health sciences/Medical research/Translational research](/browse?subjectArea=Health%20sciences%2FMedical%20research%2FTranslational%20research) # Main Understanding the human brain, normal or diseased, is one the most significant scientific challenges of the 21st century, with academic, medical, societal and economic implications. In this context, neuroimaging, and especially Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), has become an essential approach, as it allows maps of the brain anatomy, function, and structural connectivity to be obtained in patients and healthy volunteers *in situ* and non-invasively, as recognized by the Human Brain Project (now eBrain), the US Brain Initiatives and Brain Connects programs, and the China Brain Project [1]. Continuing efforts aim at boosting functional [2] and diffusion [3,4] MRI to explore the human brain *in vivo* at finer spatial resolutions [5]. By revealing at a mesoscopic scale the structural-functional links that compose the human brain (cytoarchitectonic of neuron clusters and their connections), one could bridge the gap toward both the extensive body of knowledge we already have at the microscopic scale on neurons and synapses, and the brain connectome at a macroscopic scale. This will allow us to confirm or refute our current hypotheses regarding its functioning and to generate new ones. Benefitting from a supralinear gain in sensitivity [6,7], this goal can be reached by boosting the magnetic field, B0, at which MRI systems operate. The Iseult project, originated in 2001, was based on this vision and aimed at building the first MRI magnet operating at 11.7T to investigate the human brain at an unprecedented scale. After nearly 20 years of research and development mainly led by the Department of Astrophysics, Particle Physics, Nuclear Physics and Associated Instrumentation (DAPNIA, since renamed IRFU) of CEA, the 132 tons 11.7T magnet (installation set-up shown in Figure 1) together with the scanner equipment delivered in 2021 its first images of the human brain *ex vivo* [8]. The drastic field homogeneity and temporal stability constraints for MRI were met respectively thanks to a careful design of the main superconducting coil (double pancakes) coupled with a passive iron shim, and an external power supply [9], reaching 0.9 ppm peak-to-peak over a 22 cm diameter sphere and 3 ppb/hour field drift at thermal equilibrium [8]. When increasing B0, the radiofrequency (RF) field used to excite the nuclear spins becomes more and more inhomogeneous, a long-standing and major problem in MRI due to the shortening of the wavelength of the RF waves used (500 MHz at 11.7T for the hydrogen nucleus of water), leading to severe artifactual signal variations in the images visible already on the *ex-vivo* brain [8]. We tackled this problem by deploying parallel transmission hardware and software, first developed by the laboratory at 7T and further optimized for 11.7T (Methods). The specific absorption rate (SAR), also growing with Bo [10], was tamed with virtual observation points [11] and dedicated RF pulse design algorithms [12]. Approval from the regulatory body and ethics committee to perform first tests *in vivo* on 20 healthy adult subjects was granted early 2023. Overall, for small flip angle excitations and large flip angle refocusing pulses, normalized root mean square errors (NRMSE) equal or lower than 13% thereby could be obtained over the whole brain, the latter value being the intrinsic inhomogeneity achieved at 3T with volume coils [13]. Inversion pulses remained difficult to achieve given the architecture of the coil, leaving very localized artefacts despite reaching 8% of NRMSE. Static B0 shimming was performed up to second order for each subject, using a brain mask and a quadratic programming approach yielding on average 82.7 Hz standard deviation over the brain (0.17 ppm). After B0 and RF field mapping for each subject, following the RF pulse designs T2 and T2*-weighted acquisitions were successfully performed by deploying the parallel transmission techniques (Fig. 2). The presented *in vivo* human brain images acquired at 11.7T thereby reveal good signal homogeneity consistent with the simulations, without severe RF field inhomogeneity artefacts. A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T on different volunteers. The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T, which can be traded for higher spatial (or temporal) resolutions, enabling access to fine brain details. With the limited number of subjects authorized in this first protocol, and considering the problems induced by head motion, we could obtain good quality images with resolutions up to 0.19×0.19×1 mm3 resolution (36 nl voxel volume) in about 5 min. Images indicate sufficient SNR to boost further spatial resolution or decrease acquisition time in the future. Whether human beings could withstand 1.5-hour scans at 11.7T was also uncharted territory, while vestibular effects have been reported in mice [14], as well as mild genotoxic effects with repeated or chronic field exposures [15]. This first study, therefore, proceeded with care to gather reasonable evidence for the absence of harmful effects in more realistic conditions [16,17]. Physiologic (cardiac pulsation, arterial pressure), vestibular [18] and cognitive [19,20] measurements were implemented in the protocol to verify the innocuousness of the large magnetic field on humans. Those tests were performed on the 20 subjects scanned at 11.7T and on another cohort of 20 subjects with the magnetic field switched off, to disentangle any effect from a possible psychological bias (nocebo effect). The subjects of the 0T group were not informed of the absence of the magnetic field and received the same instructions as the volunteers scanned at 11.7T. Due to the absence of gradient coil vibrations at 0T, we mimicked the sound of MRI sequences with recordings played by a loudspeaker hidden at the back of the magnet. Genotoxicity was also assessed but only for the 11.7T group with blood samples drawn before and after the 90 min exposure to perform intra-subject comparisons. Across all the tests carried out in this protocol, the statistical analysis revealed no significant differences related to field exposure (Methods). The next steps will focus on development and implementation of motion correction tools [21,22] together with highly accelerated sequences [23,24]. The deployment of more efficient RF coils [25], more receive channels count and more powerful gradients [5] is also underway to further increase performance, especially to perform high resolution fMRI and tractography. The safety and imaging data gathered in this first study confirm the applicability of MRI at 11.7T: humans can safely tolerate such intense magnetic fields while image mesoscale resolutions can be achieved on the brain in reasonable time, provided the RF field inhomogeneity problem is mitigated to return optimal SNR. It brings to the fingertips of the neuroscience community an extraordinary opportunity to discover the brain at an unprecedented scale, to explore the biological mechanisms underlying our mental life and to better identify the mechanisms at work in neurological (e.g. Alzheimer) or psychiatric diseases (depression, schizophrenia). # References 1.
